# Telehealth Statistics

January 2, 2025

Exploring the Evolution and Impact of Telehealth

### Understanding the Surge in Telemedicine

Telehealth has rapidly evolved from a niche service to a fundamental component of healthcare delivery, particularly catalyzed by the COVID-19 pandemic. This article explores the significant shifts in telehealth usage, the demographic variations in its adoption, and the consequential policy changes that have shaped the telehealth landscape. Our analysis will delve into statistics on user satisfaction, accessibility disparities, and the comparative effectiveness of telehealth versus traditional care.

## The Pandemic's Enormous Boost to Telehealth Utilization

![Exploring the Surge in Telehealth Adoption During the COVID-19 Crisis](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256e9_115aad563db404c27902b7da7250bac32ee977fa.png)

![Exploring the Surge in Telehealth Adoption During the COVID-19 Crisis](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256e9_115aad563db404c27902b7da7250bac32ee977fa.png)

### How did telehealth usage change during and after the COVID-19 pandemic?

During the COVID-19 pandemic, telehealth usage amongst office-based physicians skyrocketed from just 15.4% in 2019 to an impressive 86.5% in 2021. This transition highlights an urgent need for remote healthcare solutions during the crisis. Interestingly, medical specialists adopted telemedicine more extensively, with 27.4% reporting they used it for 50% or more of their patient interactions.

Despite the pivot to virtual care, varying levels of satisfaction emerged among different specialties. For instance, 76.7% of primary care physicians and 73.1% of medical specialists felt confident that they could provide comparable care quality through telemedicine, in contrast to just 50.6% of surgical specialists. This divergence indicates that while many physicians embraced telehealth, others remained skeptical about its effectiveness for their practice.

### What were the differences in satisfaction and perceived care quality by specialty?

Satisfaction levels also showed significant variation. Primary care physicians had a satisfaction rate of 65.5%, and medical specialists were slightly lower at 63.6%. However, surgical specialists lagged behind, with only 49.5% expressing satisfaction with telemedicine technologies. Furthermore, perceptions regarding the appropriateness of telehealth services differed, especially in the surgical field. A striking 49.7% of surgical specialists believed telemedicine was not suitable for their practice, compared to 15.5% of primary care providers and 26.7% of medical specialists. This showcases a nuanced understanding of telehealth's role in various medical contexts, with some specialties more receptive to it than others.

## Demographics of Telehealth Users

![Understanding Who Uses Telehealth: Key Demographics](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256de_d9d27146779b60b8afa66f0713309ef89945d4cb.png)

![Understanding Who Uses Telehealth: Key Demographics](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256de_d9d27146779b60b8afa66f0713309ef89945d4cb.png)

### Who are the most frequent users of telehealth services?

The demographic landscape of telehealth users reflects a diverse range of factors that influence adoption rates. Notably, **women** have emerged as the more frequent users of telehealth services compared to men. In 2021, **42.0% of women** utilized telemedicine, while only **31.7% of men** did. This suggests that gender plays a significant role in the uptake of digital health services.

Age is another critical factor affecting telehealth utilization. In 2021, **43.3% of seniors aged 65 and over** accessed telemedicine, contrasting sharply with just **29.4%** among younger adults aged 18â29. This trend indicates a growing comfort and willingness to engage with telehealth as patients age.

Furthermore, socioeconomic status significantly impacts telehealth usage. Higher income groups demonstrate a greater tendency to use these services. For instance, **40.7% of adults earning 400% or more above the Federal Poverty Level** took advantage of telemedicine, while only **33.1% of those earning below 100% of FPL** did.

Telehealth usage is more common in urban areas, where **40.3% of residents** in major metropolitan centers accessed digital healthcare, compared to just **27.5% in noncore areas**. Such patterns emphasize that while telehealth is becoming prevalent, barriers still exist related to income, education, and geographic location.

## Telehealth's Role in Chronic and Special Care

![How Telehealth Empowers Chronic Care Management](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256d6_23b420a58c73456061000cd2d9825c527c77f49a.png)

![How Telehealth Empowers Chronic Care Management](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256d6_23b420a58c73456061000cd2d9825c527c77f49a.png)

### Which populations benefit the most from telehealth?

Populations that benefit the most from telehealth include those managing chronic conditions such as cardiac issues, diabetes, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and hypertension. Telehealth plays a crucial role in enabling remote patient monitoring (RPM), which allows healthcare providers to track symptoms and vital signs in real-time. This capability not only enhances patient engagement but also contributes to improved health outcomes.

In chronic care management, RPM has been shown to reduce hospital readmissions and support patient recovery through continuous monitoring and timely interventions. For instance, oncology patients experience improved access to care and regular monitoring of their health status, which is essential given the complexities of cancer treatment.

Moreover, telehealth offers significant advantages for pregnant and postpartum women. Virtual consultations can guide them through potential complications, ensuring prompt medical attention when necessary, thus enhancing maternal and infant health.

Overall, the integration of telehealth and RPM within these populations streamlines healthcare delivery, effectively reduces costs, and enhances patient satisfaction. Telehealth stands out as a valuable tool, especially for those in specialized care models like Hospital at Home, hospice and palliative care, and prenatal/postpartum care, thereby redefining the healthcare landscape.

## Current Trends and Statistics in Telehealth Usage

![Latest 2023 Trends in Telehealth Usage and Integration](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256e1_2e9432be5489df3a41c77d85050ca7dca0409772.png)

![Latest 2023 Trends in Telehealth Usage and Integration](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256e1_2e9432be5489df3a41c77d85050ca7dca0409772.png)

### What are the 2023 telehealth usage statistics?

In 2023, telehealth usage presented a mixed picture compared to prior years. As of January, national telehealth utilization rose to 5.9% of medical claim lines, which represented a 7.3% increase from December 2022. Despite this uptick, 24% of Medicare fee-for-service users engaged with telehealth services, indicating a 5% decline from the previous year. This decline emphasizes the challenges in sustaining telehealth adoption post-pandemic.

An encouraging trend is observed in primary care settings, where telehealth remains integral; a remarkable 96% of HRSA-funded health centers have adopted telehealth to enhance service delivery. Among states, California stands out, with 46.7% of adults reporting telehealth usage in the past year, reflecting regional differences in telehealth adoption.

### Integration of telehealth in health centers

The integration of telehealth in healthcare systems has seen significant progress. As noted, over 96% of HRSA-funded health centers incorporate telehealth services into their operations, underscoring its crucial role in modern healthcare. This approach allows these centers to reach underserved populations effectively, offering remote consultations and monitoring to ensure continuity of care.

Furthermore, the legislative support for telehealth, with many states enacting laws promoting its use, indicates a robust framework for ongoing integration in clinical practice. This focus on expanding telehealth capabilities can ultimately enhance patient care and accessibility across diverse demographics and regions.

## Teletherapy Trends and Patient Satisfaction

![The Rise of Teletherapy: Current Usage and Satisfaction Levels](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256db_b9801c9de7eab4ad2437f5a3305f8ef951acf2cc.png)

![The Rise of Teletherapy: Current Usage and Satisfaction Levels](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6622e01a23f4ca686b3b8aa3/6776997d85164c4a1bc256db_b9801c9de7eab4ad2437f5a3305f8ef951acf2cc.png)

### What are the current statistics on teletherapy usage?

Teletherapy has gained immense traction in recent years. As of early 2024, **54% of Americans** reported having at least one telehealth visit, highlighting its growing acceptance. The telehealth industry is predicted to continue flourishing with an **annual growth rate of 11.83%**, reaching **over 116 million users** engaging in online consultations worldwide.

Generational trends reveal significant disparities: **68% of Millennials** are using telehealth services compared to **only 38% of Baby Boomers**. This suggests that younger generations are more open to using digital health solutions. Satisfaction levels among teletherapy users are remarkably high, with **86% reporting positive experiences** during their virtual consultations. This favorable feedback is likely a strong factor in the sustained growth of teletherapy services.

Furthermore, public interest in expanding teletherapy options is evident, as **67% of survey respondents** support legislation aimed at enhancing access to asynchronous telehealth services. Teletherapy not only offers convenience but also aims to address mental health care accessibility, making it a vital component of modern healthcare delivery.

## Assessing the Effectiveness of Telehealth Appointments

### How effective are telehealth visits compared to in-person visits?

Telehealth visits have shown remarkable effectiveness, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic. Utilization of telemedicine surged, especially in mental health services, where the number of visits nearly doubled. Studies reveal that around 67% of patients perceive video or phone consultations to be "as good or better" than face-to-face appointments.

Telehealth significantly improves accessibility, particularly for individuals in remote areas, facilitating continuity of care for chronic conditions. It serves well for follow-ups and specialties such as mental health, proving beneficial in maintaining quality care.

However, challenges persist regarding the sufficiency of physical examinations via telemedicine. While patients often report high satisfaction levels, the need for in-person visits remains for certain assessments. Yet, the overall trend shows telehealth is becoming an invaluable complement to traditional care.

### Valuation in mental health services

The integration of telehealth into mental healthcare has transformed service delivery. For example, a staggering 96% of telepsychiatry patients report satisfaction with virtual mental health services, indicating a growing acceptance and recognition of telehealthâs worth in this field. Overall, these developments highlight telemedicine's expanding role in providing effective care options.

## Telehealth Statistical Highlights from the CDC

### What are the CDC's telehealth statistics for recent years?

The CDC's telehealth statistics reveal significant trends in how telemedicine has been adopted in recent years. In 2021, an impressive **37.0%** of adults in the U.S. engaged in telemedicine services. This figure indicates a broad acceptance of remote healthcare, particularly notable among older adults and individuals with higher education levels.

However, by 2022, the utilization rate dipped to **30.1%**, signaling a decline in telehealth adoption post-pandemic. This downturn was observed across multiple demographics, with women continuing to report higher usage than men.

Geographical differences also played a role in access, with adults in regions like the Northeast (40.0%) and the West (42.4%) demonstrating the highest telemedicine usage, contrasting with significantly lower rates in the Midwest (33.3%) and the South (34.3%).

### Changes in usage post-COVID

The post-COVID landscape of telehealth highlights varying experiences across different groups. Usage notably decreased across nearly all sociodemographic categories, including age, sex, and income. For example, telehealth use among older adults dropped from **43.3%** in 2021 to **30.6%** in 2022.

This decline calls attention to the evolving landscape of healthcare delivery, influenced by factors such as technology accessibility and patient preferences. The data reflects a mixed outlook for telehealth's future as it continues to integrate into routine care.

## Telehealth's Unfolding Future in Healthcare

Telehealth has cemented its role as an accessible and often indispensable healthcare service for many individuals across diverse demographics. The ongoing policy shifts and technological advancements promise further integration and enhancement of telehealth services. However, attention to disparities in access and the balance between virtual and in-person care delivery will be crucial to ensure equitable healthcare outcomes. As telehealth continues to evolve, it holds the potential to redefine patient care, making comprehensive healthcare more accessible and efficient worldwide.

## Exploring the Surge in Telehealth Usage

### Introduction to Telehealth Trends

The landscape of healthcare has experienced substantial shifts over recent years, particularly with the rise of telehealth services. Driven by the COVID-19 pandemic, the utilization of telemedicine has not only increased significantly but has also evolved to accommodate various medical needs across diverse demographics. This exploration delves into the statistics surrounding telehealth usage, demonstrating its growth, adoption disparities, and impact on healthcare quality.

## Key Facts on the Rise of Telehealth Since COVID-19

![Explore the astonishing growth of telehealth utilization since the COVID-19 pandemic!](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071ba_d60cfc8fb1ece444d0817d969176bdfbbd9305bc.png)

## The Rise of Telehealth Since COVID-19

![Discover how COVID-19 has transformed telehealth outlook and adoption rates.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b4_9d9bd60ab5ffa1e0b691e564d48b26320aead23c.png)

![Discover how COVID-19 has transformed telehealth outlook and adoption rates.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b4_9d9bd60ab5ffa1e0b691e564d48b26320aead23c.png)

### Has telehealth increased since COVID-19?

Telehealth has indeed experienced a significant boost since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Initially, telehealth visits among Medicare recipients skyrocketed from approximately 5 million to over 53 million, illustrating a remarkable rise in its acceptance. A national study highlighted a 766% increase in telemedicine encounters during the early months of the pandemic, marking a transformative moment in healthcare delivery.

This surge was not only significant in numbers but also reflected a changing attitude towards telehealth. Following the pandemic's peak, a survey indicated that 80% of physicians plan to continue utilizing telehealth services, emphasizing its integration into modern healthcare practices. Moreover, a CDC report from 2021 noted that 37% of adults had utilized telemedicine services within the preceding year, pointing towards a continuing trend of acceptance and reliance on this method of care.

### What were the adoption rates among Medicare recipients during the pandemic?

Adoption rates among Medicare recipients surged dramatically during the pandemic. The early 2020 period witnessed telehealth visits soar, ultimately reaching millions as older adults sought accessible healthcare options amid social distancing mandates. This shift was a groundbreaking move for a demographic that had limited telehealth engagement prior to COVID-19, making it an essential tool for patient care.

### What are physician intentions regarding telehealth?

Physicians are showing strong intentions to continue with telehealth services even after the pandemic. This ongoing commitment is rooted in favorable experiences reported by both doctors and patients alike. As healthcare systems adapt, telehealth appears set to remain a cornerstone of medical practice, enhancing care delivery for a diverse patient population.

## Current Teletherapy Usage Statistics

![Get insights on current teletherapy usage statistics as telehealth becomes a mainstay!](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b1_85922a9a9e843ad19aa82dad6ed09e510bd30c4a.png)

![Get insights on current teletherapy usage statistics as telehealth becomes a mainstay!](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b1_85922a9a9e843ad19aa82dad6ed09e510bd30c4a.png)

### What are the current statistics on teletherapy usage?

As of early 2024, teletherapy has become an integral part of healthcare delivery. Approximately **54% of Americans** have participated in at least one telehealth visit, with nearly **38% of these visits** pertaining to medical or mental health needs. This growth reflects an increasing recognition of the importance of mental health services, with **92% of Americans** acknowledging its seriousness.

A significant **60% of survey respondents** expressed openness to using teletherapy for mental health services. This openness is pivotal as the demand for mental health support continues to rise. In fact, there was a notable **26% increase** in women seeking mental health treatment in 2022, indicating that teletherapy is becoming a preferred option for many.

### Satisfaction rates among teletherapy users

Satisfaction rates among teletherapy users are impressively high, with more than **86% of individuals** reporting positive experiences. These numbers highlight not only the effectiveness of teletherapy but also the comfort and convenience it provides for patients. This increased satisfaction may contribute to the sustained interest in telehealth services even after the pandemic.

### Growth rate of the telehealth industry focusing on mental health

The telehealth industry is experiencing significant growth, currently at an **annual rate of 11.83%**. Projections indicate that over **116 million users** are engaging in online consultations globally. This expansion underscores not just a temporary shift but a long-term evolution in how mental health services are delivered, with teletherapy leading the way in accessible care options.

## The Effectiveness of Telehealth Consultations

![Uncover the effectiveness of telehealth consultations and patient satisfaction!](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071a5_200c888508af6949b6e71e230ff013b4215e97f0.png)

![Uncover the effectiveness of telehealth consultations and patient satisfaction!](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071a5_200c888508af6949b6e71e230ff013b4215e97f0.png)

### How effective are telehealth visits?

Telehealth visits have proven to be quite effective, especially during and after the COVID-19 pandemic, when their utilization increased significantly by 766%. Surveys reveal that a majority of patients (67%) find telehealth visits to be as good, or better, than in-person visits. Patients often cite ease of use and cost-effectiveness as major benefits of this method. Outcomes from telemedicine are comparable to traditional care for conditions like asthma and chronic heart failure, leading to enhanced patient satisfaction and improved access to care, especially in underserved areas.

### Patient satisfaction with telehealth

Satisfaction levels with telehealth are notably high, with approximately 83% of patients expressing contentment with the telehealth experience. Moreover, many appreciate the reduced need for travel and time savings, which averaged 51 minutes per visit. Telemedicine has also been associated with a 67% reduction in emergency department visits, highlighting its effectiveness in managing health conditions.

### Comparison of telehealth outcomes to in-person visits

While telehealth has demonstrated significant efficacy, some challenges remain, particularly regarding disparities in access. Individuals without insurance have a notably lower telehealth usage rate of 9.4%, contrasted with higher rates among those covered by Medicaid and Medicare, which stand at 28.3% and 26.8%, respectively.

Additionally, there are concerns surrounding potential gaps in care and the quality of telehealth services, particularly in direct-to-consumer scenarios. The varying effectiveness of telehealth across different demographic groups raises questions about equity and overall healthcare delivery. However, continuing research and adaptations are expected to optimize telehealth practices for different medical conditions moving forward.

### Challenges and disparities in telemedicine effectiveness

Despite its advantages, telehealth faces ongoing challenges such as technological limitations and varying insurance reimbursements. Notably, Black and Hispanic individuals were less likely to access video-based services, which may affect outcomes. Monitoring these disparities and enhancing access is crucial as telemedicine secures its place in modern healthcare delivery systems.

## Demographics and Telehealth Utilization Patterns

### Telehealth usage by age, gender, and race

Telehealth has seen varying usage rates across different demographics. Among adults aged 65 and over, **43.3%** have utilized telehealth services, showcasing a strong adoption in older populations compared to only **29.4%** of younger adults aged 18-29. Gender disparities also exist; **42.0%** of women engage in telemedicine as opposed to **31.7%** of men. Racially, **39.2%** of non-Hispanic White adults reported using telehealth, in contrast to **32.8%** of Hispanics and **33.1%** of non-Hispanic Blacks.

### Impact of education and income on telehealth adoption

Education level significantly influences telehealth utilization. Approximately **43.2%** of college graduates reported using telehealth services, while participation drops to **28.7%** among those without a high school diploma. Economic factors further play a role; individuals earning above **400%** of the federal poverty line exhibit a **40.7%** usage rate compared to **33.1%** for those below **100%** of the federal poverty line. This shows that both educational attainment and income level heavily affect telehealth adoption.

### Disparities in telehealth access

Access to telehealth is not uniformly distributed. Racial demographics reveal that Hispanic and Black individuals have lower rates of usage compared to White individuals. Furthermore, urban areas report higher usage rates of **40.3%**, whereas rural communities lag behind at **27.5%**. Such disparities highlight the need for improving access to telehealth services across varying demographics, ensuring equitable healthcare access for all populations.

## Specialty-Wise Variations in Telehealth Utilization

### How does telehealth utilization vary by specialty?

Telehealth usage varies significantly across medical specialties, demonstrating both wide adoption and notable gaps. According to reports, psychiatry leads in telehealth adoption, achieving an impressive 98.3% utilization during the July to September 2020 period. In contrast, dermatology trailed significantly with only 3.2% of visits occurring virtually.

In 2023, mental health remained at the forefront, with 36.8% of mental health visits conducted through telehealth. This is followed by infectious disease specialists at 10.6%, and obstetricians, also at 10%. However, recovery trends post-COVID-19 have shown that while telehealth usage remains above pre-pandemic levels, some specialties face challenges that limit their engagement with telehealth. For instance, fields like ophthalmology and podiatry struggle with telehealth application, with usage dipping below 1%, primarily due to their requirement for hands-on assessments.

Disparities in telehealth access also persist, particularly affecting African American and male patients, who are less likely to utilize these services compared to other demographic groups. Overall, telehealth is solidifying its place as a vital component in various specialties, although its effectiveness and adoption rates fluctuate based on the specific medical needs and care delivery models.

## Provider Utilization of Telehealth

![Examine how healthcare providers embrace telehealth in their practice.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b7_6d694ad1ac30726eb35e35392e6d0a8810a0f912.png)

![Examine how healthcare providers embrace telehealth in their practice.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6627fd62e242d50407cfe155/6749c8881bb9eaeb187071b7_6d694ad1ac30726eb35e35392e6d0a8810a0f912.png)

### What percentage of providers use telehealth?

As of 2022, **74%** of physicians reported using telehealth services in their practices, reflecting a significant increase from previous years. In 2018, only **14.3%** of physicians had the capability to videoconference with patients, which surged to **66.3%** by 2022. Nearly half (**49.8%**) of physicians used telehealth to diagnose or treat patients, up from **15.6%** in 2018. These changes highlight the rapid evolution of telehealth capabilities in medical practices.

### Growth in telehealth capabilities among healthcare providers

The rise in telehealth usage is notable across various specialties. For example, **27.4%** of medical specialists utilized telemedicine for more than half of their patient visits by 2021. This was significantly higher compared to primary care providers at **14.7%** and surgical specialists at only **5.5%**. The pandemic accelerated these trends, with more than **80%** of physicians planning to continue utilizing telehealth after the pandemic.

### Integration of telehealth into chronic disease management

Telehealth has proven especially effective in managing chronic conditions such as diabetes and hypertension. **54.9%** of physicians indicated that their practices implemented telehealth tools for chronic disease management. This integration supports better patient follow-up, leading to improved health outcomes. Patients attending telehealth visits were reported to be **twice as likely** to adhere to their care plans compared to those who had in-person visits, underscoring the effectiveness of remote care solutions in todayâs healthcare landscape.

## Conclusion on Telehealth Statistics

In summary, telehealth has undergone transformative growth and acceptance, fundamentally reshaping how healthcare is delivered. The statistics presented highlight the varied adoption across different demographics and specialties, signaling both progress and challenges. While telehealth continues to demonstrate significant effectiveness and satisfaction, particularly in areas like mental health and chronic disease management, disparities in access remain a critical issue. As the healthcare landscape evolves, telemedicine is poised to play an increasingly vital role. Ongoing adjustments in policy, technology, and training will be essential to maximize its benefits and ensure equitable access for all.

# New Telehealth Analysis Shows Sustained Demand for Mental Health Services

#### Published by [**Spencer Fortier**](https://civhc.org/author/sfortiercivhc-org/ "Spencer Fortier") on February 11, 2025 February 13, 2025

The Center for Improving Value in Health Care (CIVHC) recently released its sixth update to the [Telehealth Services Utilization Analysis](https://www.civhc.org/covid-19/telehealth-services-analysis/), which now includes data from 2019 through 2023. The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated the adoption of telehealth services, reshaping health care delivery in Colorado. Using claims data from the [Colorado All Payer Claims Database (CO APCD)](https://civhc.org/get-data/whats-in-the-co-apcd/), the Telehealth Services Analysis provides critical insights into this transformation. The latest data shows that nearly five years after the onset of COVID-19, telehealth remains a vital source of care, especially for Coloradans accessing mental health services.

The analysis shows that while overall telehealth usage has decreased from its initial pandemic peak, demand for services with behavioral health and primary care providers remain much higher than pre-pandemic levels. In 2020, telehealth services with primary care providers peaked at 69 services per 1,000 people per month but has stabilized to approximately 15 services per 1,000 people. Telehealth services with behavioral health providers have consistently dominated telehealth visits, averaging around 35 services per 1,000 people monthly since 2020.

![](https://civhc.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Telehealth-Services-Analysis-2025-Utilization-Trends-1024x416.jpg)

Across all provider types, telehealth visits for mental health related reasons continue to trend upward representing 47% of all visits in 2020 and 58% of all visits in 2023. Telehealth is crucial for providing access to mental health care, and helps reduce barriers like provider shortages, travel time, and stigma. In 2023, general anxiety was the most common telehealth mental health diagnosis, making up 18% of all visits, followed by depression (9%), post-traumatic stress (6%), adjustment (5%), and anxiety (5%).

![](https://civhc.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Telehealth-Services-Analysis-2025-Top-Diagnosis-1024x440.jpg)

This latest release also explored geographic differences between telehealth use in rural and urban areas. In rural communities, Medicaid-insured patients received the most telehealth visits (40%), and in urban areas commercially insured patients had the most visits (42%). Approximately 86% of people in Colorado live in urban counties, and these residents made up 92% of all of the telehealth visits statewide. Despite some geographic differences, mental health conditions emerged as the leading diagnosis for both rural (83%) and urban (86%) residents, followed by endocrine and nutritional conditions.

# What to expect in telehealth in 2023? Here are 5 predictions

About 25% of patients used telehealth last year, far exceeding the 5% who accessed care this way before the pandemic. And as the budding field evolves, that share is expected to continue to rise in the coming years.

### The AMA is advocating for you

See our real-world impact on issues critical to patients and physicians.

During an [AMA Telehealth Immersion Program](https://www.ama-assn.org/practice-management/digital/ama-telehealth-immersion-program) webinar, experts shared their views and perspectives on how a system propelled to the forefront of medicine evolved in 2022 and where it is headed in 2023.

Telehealth usage spiked to 70% in 2020, but that happened in a U.S. health care system that wasn’t built or optimized for such an influx of patients.

The usability wasn’t there and, culturally, patients and physicians who weren’t fully ready to move to virtual care were happy to go back to in-person visits, internist Eyal Zimlichman, MD, said during the webinar. He is the chief innovation and transformation officer at Sheba Medical Center, Israel’s largest hospital, and previously served as an adviser to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology.

So, the nation’s health care system will experience an evolution—not a revolution—of telehealth, he said.

“We are going to see a slow rise from the 20% to 25% we are currently [seeing], moving up maybe toward that 70% again, but in a more natural course where the usability is improving and where the culture is  slowly getting there,” Dr. Zimlichman said. “It’s going to take another five years or so … to see that go up to the 50% to 70% rate that we’ve seen” during the early part of the pandemic.

Supporting telehealth is an essential component of the [AMA Recovery Plan for America’s Physicians](https://www.ama-assn.org/amaone/ama-recovery-plan-america-s-physicians).

[Telehealth is critical to the future of health care](https://www.ama-assn.org/amaone/ama-recovery-plan-america-s-physicians-supporting-telehealth), which is why the AMA continues to lead the charge to aggressively expand telehealth policy, research and resources to ensure physician practice sustainability and fair payment.

### Half the dues, all the AMA benefits!

#### Related Coverage

## 5 predictions for 2023

Dr. Zimlichman was joined by panelists Lisa Lavin, founder and CEO of Ōmcare Inc., a digital health care company offering a customizable home health platform for organizations, and Tory Cenaj, founder and publisher of *Telehealth and Medicine Today*.

### Stay up to date on AI

Follow the latest news on AI and its applications and effects for health care—delivered to your inbox.

![Health care AI subscribe](/sites/ama-assn.org/files/styles/membership_promo_image/public/2024-10/health-care-ai-subscribe.jpg?itok=KLhd8hU_)

Here are their predictions for telehealth in 2023.

**Telehealth will continue to evolve into something that’s easier to use.** Expect to see more effort and money put into the usability of telehealth solutions. Patients and physicians have had a taste of how technology can revolutionize health care, but the technology needs to continue to improve to expand the number of people who use telehealth on a regular basis.

**The field will see steps toward aggregated, all-in-one technology**. Look for some “eyebrow-raising” joint ventures between technology and health care professionals that are going to help take steps toward more seamless technology.

**Chronic disease management will see strides**. Medicine has been focused on pharma when it comes to managing chronic illness. But it is much more complicated than just pills and that’s where digital health tools will come into play. Expect to see more wearable technology and sensors, along with chat boxes and symptom-management tools to manage an area that is one of the biggest buckets of cost and morbidity and mortality in health care.

#### Related Coverage

**Government funding and regulation for telehealth will continue to be a hot topic.** Telehealth will only continue to grow if physicians and other health care providers are being paid properly for the services. Look for this, along with regulatory issues such as licensing and treating patients across state lines to get more attention in 2023.

**More capital will flow to innovators in the digital health care arena.** Investors have capital to deploy into health care and a number of health care companies are sitting on large capital reserves. Expect more capital to go to innovators in virtual care and digital health solutions.

Learn more about the AMA’s new “[Blueprint for Optimizing Digitally Enabled Care](https://www.ama-assn.org/system/files/ama-future-health-report.pdf)” (PDF), which is designed to help bridge the digital health chasm as the pace of digital health progress in medicine does not yet match the technology’s potential.

[The Telehealth Immersion Program](https://www.ama-assn.org/practice-management/digital/ama-telehealth-immersion-program) is part of the [AMA STEPS Forward™ Innovation Academy](https://www.ama-assn.org/practice-management/sustainability/ama-steps-forward-webinar-series), which enables physicians to learn from peers and experts and discover ways to implement and optimize time-saving practice innovation strategies.

This article presents a comprehensive analysis of teletherapy statistics, offering insights into its growth, adoption, and impact on mental health care delivery. Chapter 1: The Growth and Adoption of Teletherapy
-------------------------------------------------

The teletherapy landscape has experienced unprecedented growth, particularly accelerated by global events and technological advancements. This section examines the rapid expansion of teletherapy services and their increasing adoption among various demographics.

### 1.1 Industry Growth and Market Projections

The telehealth industry, encompassing teletherapy, is witnessing remarkable growth. As of 2024, the sector is expanding at an annual rate of 11.83%. This growth is not just a temporary spike but part of a long-term trend reshaping the healthcare landscape.

Looking ahead, the future appears even more promising. The global telemedicine market, which includes teletherapy services, is projected to reach a staggering USD 590.9 billion by 2032. This represents a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 25.7% from 2022, indicating sustained and substantial growth over the next decade.

This exponential growth is driven by various factors, including increased awareness of mental health issues, technological advancements, and the convenience offered by teletherapy services.

### 1.2 User Adoption and Demographics

The adoption of teletherapy services has seen a significant uptick in recent years. In 2024, over 116 million users globally are engaging in online doctor consultations. This marks a substantial increase from 57 million users in 2019, demonstrating the rapid acceptance and integration of telehealth services into mainstream healthcare practices.

When it comes to teletherapy specifically, the statistics are equally impressive.

*   Nearly 38% of Americans have utilized telehealth services for medical or mental health needs.

This high adoption rate indicates a growing comfort with and trust in digital health solutions.

Interestingly, there’s a notable generational divide in telehealth usage:

Younger adults, particularly those aged 18-29, show a significantly higher inclination towards using telehealth services (66%) compared to older adults (36%). This disparity suggests that as younger generations age, the overall adoption of teletherapy is likely to increase further.

*   This gender difference extends to mental health treatment more broadly, with a 26% increase in women receiving mental health treatment in 2022 compared to the previous year, while men saw a 34.71% increase.

### 1.3 Patient Satisfaction and Preferences

The growing adoption of teletherapy is supported by high satisfaction rates among users.

*   Consistently, over 86% of teletherapy users report satisfaction with their experiences since tracking began.

This high satisfaction rate is a crucial factor in the sustained growth of the industry.

*   Moreover, a survey indicated that 60% of respondents would consider using teletherapy for mental health services.

This openness to digital mental health solutions suggests a significant potential for further growth in the sector.

However, it’s worth noting that preferences for therapy formats vary:

Only about 44.5% of patients preferred in-person psychotherapy over digital options like self-guided or expert-guided treatments. This shift in preference towards digital solutions underscores the changing landscape of mental health care delivery.

Chapter 2: Impact of Teletherapy on Healthcare Access and Delivery
------------------------------------------------------------------

Teletherapy has significantly altered the landscape of healthcare access and delivery, particularly in the realm of mental health services. This section explores how teletherapy is addressing gaps in mental health care access and changing the way healthcare is delivered.

### 2.1 Improving Mental Health Care Access

One of the most significant impacts of teletherapy has been its role in improving access to mental health care.

*   Despite 92% of Americans acknowledging mental health issues as serious problems, only 44% believe they can access necessary mental health care. Teletherapy is helping to bridge this gap.

The convenience and accessibility of teletherapy services have made it possible for more individuals to seek mental health support. This is particularly crucial given the increasing awareness and demand for mental health services, especially in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic.

### 2.2 Teletherapy Usage Patterns

The adoption of teletherapy services has been widespread, with 54% of Americans having had at least one telehealth visit by early 2024. What’s more encouraging is that 89% expressed satisfaction with their last appointment, indicating high quality of care delivered through digital platforms.

Teletherapy is being used for a variety of health needs:

*   About 69% of respondents reported using telehealth services for common illnesses and infections, while 38% have utilized these services for medical or mental health needs.

This diversity in usage demonstrates the versatility of teletherapy platforms in addressing various health concerns.

### 2.3 Time and Cost Efficiency

One of the key advantages of teletherapy is its efficiency in terms of time and potentially cost.

*   Telemedicine can save patients an average of about 121 minutes per in-person visit by reducing travel and waiting times by approximately 31%.

This time-saving aspect is particularly beneficial for individuals with chronic conditions who require frequent consultations.

The time efficiency of teletherapy not only improves patient convenience but also potentially increases the capacity of mental health professionals to see more patients, thereby improving overall access to care.

### 2.4 Legislative Support and Insurance Coverage

The growth of teletherapy is also being supported by legislative initiatives.

*   Two-thirds (67%) of Americans support legislation aimed at expanding access to asynchronous telehealth services.

This public support is crucial for driving policy changes that can further facilitate the adoption and integration of teletherapy into mainstream healthcare.

However, insurance coverage for telehealth [services](https://crowncounseling.com/services/) remains a complex issue.

*   As of 2021, the prevalence of [insurers](https://crowncounseling.com/insurance/) offering telehealth services varied significantly by region, with many still lacking comprehensive coverage options.

This variability in insurance coverage presents both a challenge and an opportunity for further growth in the teletherapy sector.

Chapter 4: Teletherapy and the COVID-19 Pandemic
------------------------------------------------

The COVID-19 pandemic has been a significant catalyst for the adoption and expansion of teletherapy services. This section examines the impact of the pandemic on teletherapy usage and its long-term implications for mental health care delivery.

### 4.1 Surge in Telehealth Adoption During the Pandemic

The pandemic necessitated a rapid shift towards remote healthcare options, including teletherapy.

*   During the peak of the COVID-19 crisis, over 97% of healthcare professionals utilized telemedicine services.

This unprecedented adoption rate was driven by the need to continue providing care while minimizing in-person [contact](https://crowncounseling.com/contact/).

### 4.2 Increased Mental Health Awareness and Demand

The pandemic has significantly raised awareness about mental health issues, contributing to increased demand for teletherapy services. The stress, isolation, and uncertainty associated with the pandemic led to a heightened focus on mental well-being, driving more individuals to seek psychological support.

This increased awareness is reflected in the growth of mental health treatment rates:

Gender Increase in Mental Health Treatment (2022 vs. Previous Year)
Women 26%
Men 34.71%

These substantial increases in treatment rates indicate a growing willingness to seek mental health support, with teletherapy playing a crucial role in meeting this increased demand.

### 4.3 Long-Term Impact on Teletherapy Adoption

While the initial surge in teletherapy usage was driven by necessity, the convenience and effectiveness of these services have led to sustained adoption even as pandemic restrictions have eased.

*   As of early 2024, 54% of Americans have had at least one telehealth visit, with 89% expressing satisfaction with their last appointment.

These high satisfaction rates suggest that the shift towards teletherapy is not merely a temporary response to the pandemic but a long-term change in how mental health care is accessed and delivered.

### 4.4 Teletherapy’s Role in Crisis Response

The pandemic highlighted teletherapy’s potential as a crucial tool in crisis response. The ability to rapidly scale up mental health support without the need for physical infrastructure proved invaluable during lockdowns and periods of social distancing.

This experience has underscored the importance of maintaining and further developing teletherapy capabilities to enhance resilience in future public health crises.

Chapter 5: Technological Innovations and Future Trends in Teletherapy
---------------------------------------------------------------------

As teletherapy continues to evolve, technological innovations are playing a crucial role in shaping its future. This section explores emerging technologies, future trends, and their potential impact on the delivery of mental health services.

### 5.1 Emerging Technologies in Teletherapy

Emerging technologies are transforming mental healthcare delivery, making it more accessible and personalized. Some key innovations include:

1.   Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning: These technologies are being used to develop chatbots for initial screening, personalized treatment recommendations, and mood tracking.
2.   Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR): VR and AR are being integrated into [therapy sessions for immersive exposure](https://crowncounseling.com/modalities/exposure-therapy/) therapy and relaxation techniques.
3.   Wearable Devices: Smartwatches and other wearables are being used to monitor physiological indicators of [stress and anxiety](https://crowncounseling.com/anxiety-therapy), providing real-time data to therapists.
4.   Mobile Apps: Mental health apps are becoming increasingly sophisticated, offering features like guided meditation, [cognitive behavioral therapy](https://crowncounseling.com/modalities/cognitive-behavioral-therapy/) exercises, and mood journals.

### 5.2 Digital Health Revenue Projections

The growing integration of technology in mental health care is reflected in the projected revenue for digital health services.

*   In North America alone, revenue from digital health is expected to reach approximately USD 26.59 billion by the end of 2023.

This substantial revenue projection underscores the significant economic impact of digital health solutions, including teletherapy services.

### 5.3 Future Job Market Growth in Mental Health Services

The expansion of teletherapy is expected to drive growth in mental health-related job markets. Projections indicate:

### 5.4 Challenges and Opportunities in Teletherapy Innovation

While technological innovations offer exciting possibilities for teletherapy, they also present challenges:

1.   Digital Divide: Ensuring equitable access to teletherapy services across different socioeconomic groups remains a significant challenge.
2.   Data Privacy and Security: As teletherapy platforms handle sensitive personal information, maintaining robust data protection measures is crucial.
3.   Integration with Traditional Care: Seamlessly integrating teletherapy with in-person care models presents both a challenge and an opportunity for improving overall mental health care delivery.
4.   Regulatory Adaptation: As technology evolves, regulations will need to adapt to ensure patient safety and quality of care in teletherapy services.

### 5.5 Patient Engagement in Virtual Sessions

Maintaining engagement during virtual therapy sessions remains a challenge, especially after prolonged [screen time](https://crowncounseling.com/statistics/screen-time/) throughout the day. Innovative approaches to improve engagement include:

*   Interactive therapy tools and games
*   Short, focused sessions
*   Combination of synchronous and asynchronous communication methods

As teletherapy continues to evolve, addressing these engagement challenges will be crucial for ensuring the effectiveness of virtual mental health interventions.

Chapter 6: Economic and Workforce Impact of Teletherapy
-------------------------------------------------------

The rise of teletherapy is not only transforming healthcare delivery but also significantly impacting the economy and workforce. This section examines the economic implications and workforce trends associated with the growth of teletherapy services.

### 6.1 Telehealth Sector Employment

The telehealth sector, which includes teletherapy, has become a significant employer.

As of 2024, the sector employs around 6 million professionals. This represents a substantial portion of the healthcare workforce and underscores the sector’s growing economic importance.

The addition of over 393,000 jobs in the past year alone indicates rapid growth and job creation within the telehealth industry. This trend is likely to continue as the demand for teletherapy and other telehealth services continues to rise.

### 6.2 Economic Impact of Time Savings

One of the significant economic benefits of teletherapy is the time saved by patients. Telemedicine can save patients an average of about 121 minutes per in-person visit by reducing travel and waiting times by approximately 31%. This time saving translates into economic benefits in several ways:

1.   Increased Productivity: Patients can allocate the saved time to work or other productive activities.
2.   Reduced Transportation Costs: Fewer in-person visits mean lower transportation expenses for patients.
3.   Lower Opportunity Costs: Less time spent on healthcare visits can lead to reduced childcare needs or other associated costs.

### 6.3 Market Growth and Investment Opportunities

The projected growth of the global telemedicine market to USD 590.9 billion by 2032 represents significant investment opportunities. This growth is attracting venture capital and driving innovation in the sector. Areas of investment include:

*   Teletherapy platforms and apps
*   AI-driven [mental health solutions](https://crowncounseling.com/statistics/single-parent-mental-health/)
*   [Virtual reality therapy](https://crowncounseling.com/modalities/virtual-reality/) tools
*   Wearable devices for mental health monitoring

### 6.4 Impact on Traditional Healthcare Workforce

The rise of teletherapy is also impacting the traditional healthcare workforce:

1.   Skill Adaptation: [Mental health professionals are adapting their skills to provide effective](https://crowncounseling.com/statistics/art-therapy-statistics/) therapy in virtual environments.
2.   New Roles: New job roles are emerging, such as telehealth coordinators and digital health specialists.
3.   Geographic Flexibility: Teletherapy allows mental health professionals to work with patients across wider geographic areas, potentially addressing shortages in underserved regions.
